data.title: Nordex: Maybe It Is Time To Take A Profit (Downgrade To Hold)
data.description:
Notes: Unless indicated otherwise, amounts in the article are in EUR. At the current exchange rate, one euro is approximately 1.14 U.S. dollars. All per-share figures refer to the NRDXF ticker and are in EUR.

Investment Thesis

Nordex Group SE (OTCPK:NRDXF, OTC:NRXXY) is a manufacturer of onshore wind turbines, headquartered in Hamburg, Germany. Nordex has an annual revenue of almost EUR 8 billion and over 57 GW of installed wind power capacity across more than 40 markets. Nordex has a strong focus on Europe, not a bad thing in times of constant tariff discussions. Europe accounted for 87% (in megawatts) of installations carried out in Q2 2025, Latin America for 10%, and the "Rest of the World" region for 2%.

I first covered Nordex here on Seeking Alpha in February and gave a buy recommendation (Nordex: Q4 Financial Results Could Be The Catalyst For A Turnaround). This has worked out fairly well. Shares are up almost 90% since then (in US dollars):

Nordex had not been profitable from 2019 to 2023, losing between EUR -72.6 million (2019) and EUR -497.8 million (2022) per year. However, I thought the company was making steady progress towards profitability. I expected Nordex to be profitable in Q4 and also the full FY 2024. I also forecasted positive FCF for the year. All that came to fruition, and subsequently, the stock has rallied.

Nordex announced its Q2 2025 financial results on July 28, and they were again strong. Key positives were an order intake of 2.3 GW, substantial YoY growth, and FCF of EUR 145 million.

Still, I am downgrading to Hold and have sold my position. There might be between 10% and 20% upside for the shares left, but I do not want to chase this. The German market seems overdue for a correction, and, in my view, chances are high that this will happen before the end of October. The DAX index was up 20% (on a total return basis) in 2023, 19% in 2024, and is up 21% YTD. The TecDAX, which includes Nordex, was up 15% in 2023, only 3% in 2024, and is up 10% YTD. However, the poor TecDAX performance in 2024 is distorted by a list of companies - like SMA Solar (OTCPK:SMTGF, OTCPK:SMTGY) or AIXTRON (OTCPK:AIXXF) (OTCPK:AIIXY) - that have retreated by more than 50%.

The risk-reward ratio no longer looks favorable to me. Of course, investors holding Nordex shares with a different entry point might see this differently. I am still convinced that long-term prospects for Nordex are positive, but I see some volatility in the short- to mid-term.

Let’s look at the Q2 20025 results.

Q2 2025 financial results

They were strong and above expectations, especially regarding profitability and free cash flow. The only disappointment was that sales were just EUR 1.87 billion, only a marginal YoY increase from EUR 1.86 billion in Q2 2024. Gross revenue, which includes changes in inventories, experienced a 3.1% rise, totaling EUR 1.9 billion, up from EUR 1.8 billion in the prior-year quarter. Nordex secured 2,310 MW of order intake in its Projects segment, an impressive 81.7% growth from the 1,271 MW recorded in Q2 2024. The service segment's order intake for Q2 2025 was EUR 438 million, slightly down from EUR 503.4 million in Q2 2024. At the end of the quarter, the total value of new orders, primarily from project business, amounted to EUR 2.2 billion in Q2 2025, a considerable increase from EUR 1.2 billion at the end of Q2 2024.

Profitability saw a notable improvement, with EBITDA increasing by 64.3% to EUR 108.2 million, compared to EUR 65.8 million in Q2 2024. This resulted in an improved EBITDA margin of 5.8%, up from 3.5% in Q2 2024. Similarly, EBIT for Q2 2025 was EUR 65.6 million, representing an EBIT margin of 3.5%. This marks a substantial YoY increase from EUR 22.3 million and a 1.2% EBIT margin in Q2 2024. Nordex SE also reported a strong net income of EUR 31 million in Q2 2025, a significant leap from EUR 0.5 million in the corresponding quarter last year.

Despite the significant profitability improvements, the share price rally in 2025 has made the stock quite expensive at the moment. As of July 2025, Nordex SE's trailing twelve-month P/E ratio is over 160. For the full fiscal year 2025, the estimated forward P/E ratio is around 30.

Balance sheet remains strong with a considerable net cash position

Nordex generated positive free cash flow of EUR 145.1 million, marking a significant increase of 54.4% compared to EUR 94.0 million in Q2 2024, primarily driven by its operational performance. This means the balance sheet remains strong, with a considerable net cash position. When I wrote my initial article, the net cash position was EUR 583 million, which came to 20.7% of the market cap. At the end of Q2 2025, the net cash position increased to EUR 942 million, which is now (even after the stock has rallied) still 18.5% of the market cap. Sooner or later, Nordex will have to use that cash for something, and that could be a positive future catalyst for the stock.

Nordex has reconfirmed its guidance for the full fiscal year 2025. The company anticipates consolidated sales to range between EUR 7.4 billion and EUR 7.9 billion (2024: EUR 7.3 billion), with an EBITDA margin expected to be between 5.0% and 7.0% (2024: 4.1%).

Risks

Key risks for Nordex are possible supply chain disruptions and a volatile political environment.

Nordex does little business in the US (but has growth aspirations). Therefore, neither tariffs nor Trump’s repeated tantrums against wind energy can do much harm.

As with the US, the risk from China and also from Chinese manufacturers is limited. Nordex does not operate in China. Chinese wind turbine manufacturers will probably not make significant progress in Europe in the short or mid-term (the long-term is a different story, though). The political climate is currently not supportive of Chinese manufacturers. The European Union has already launched an inquiry into Chinese suppliers of wind turbines under the Foreign Subsidies Regulation.

However, possibly shifting politics in Europe are a risk investors need to be aware of. In June, the French National Assembly, the lower house of parliament, voted on the adoption of a controversial amendment that proposed a moratorium on the development of new wind turbine and solar panel projects. While the bill was rejected with a significant majority, the debate underscores the ongoing political risks. Something similar is happening in the UK, where the Reform party explicitly states its intention to scrap the UK's net-zero target entirely. New orders in Q2 2025 were primarily driven by strong markets in Germany, Türkiye, and Latvia. Europe accounted for 87% (in megawatts) of new installations. Therefore, any negative developments in Europe regarding renewable energy will most likely affect Nordex.

Conclusion

Nordex reported Q2 2025 financial results that were above expectations. However, shares are up almost 90% since my buy recommendation in February, and they are starting to look expensive. In my view, odds are high that the German market will experience a correction in Q3, adding to the worsening risk/reward ratio. Therefore, I am downgrading Nordex to Hold and recommending that investors consider waiting for a lower entry point to start a new position.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Medical Properties Trust: Uncertainty Remains For Now, But The Patient Is Recovering
data.description:
Introduction

Here we are again, back at Medical Properties Trust (MPW). Since we are talking about one of the most turbulent REITs at this moment, frequent covering of the stock is important. Even though some of you would love to see me move on. Something that eventually will happen, but not yet!

So without further ado, I would like to share my thoughts on the Q2 2025 earnings release. It is important to understand that very soon, Medical Properties Trust will put out a new press release regarding the Prospect Medical Holdings chapter 11 bankruptcy process. I highly recommend catching up on this news. I've written about it a couple of days ago. To put it simply, Prospect is in the process of selling its operations in MPW's hospitals/behavioral facilities. Since no Prospect-related revenue is currently being reported in MPW's numbers, I believe that resolving this issue in the near future will be a net-positive going forward from a cash flow perspective. But we are talking about Medical Properties Trust, so we have to see for ourselves first.

In the mean time, I want to share with you my take on the Q2 2025 report. So let's begin.

The Q2 2025 Report

First I want to walk you through all the important quarter-over-quarter changes, to see how the cash flows from operations are trending thus far. First of all, we see no change in normalized funds from operations per share. This is a preferable starting point for REIT. It normalizes the numbers for one-time events that would otherwise skew the underlying performance of a REIT.

Regarding the share-based compensation. Since management is heavily compensated in shares, volatility in share price can lead to volatility in the reported earnings. When you take the total share-based compensation expense using grant date fair value for all awards (including cash-settled awards), you arrive at $18.5 million for the first six months ended June 30, 2025. I mention this because, in my humble opinion, management is overpaid when you look at the negative value creation that long-term investors have experienced in recent years. But it's important to keep a cool mind. In the medium term, there are good reasons to trust management. Let me explain...

In my article that was published after the Q1 2025 report, I highlighted the fact that the management compensation plan has changed. I share the most important part below. What you see is that management has an incentive to reach a share price of $7. When the stock hits that price level, management will receive a lot of their stock-based compensation. Because – obviously – we as shareholders will also benefit from this market value per share, I have a strong case to at least stick to (the bulk) of my shares until that price level is reached.

But let's dive a bit deeper into the stagnant Normalized Funds From Operations [NFFO] during Q2 2025. Because there is a valid reason why this number didn't change this quarter. Before I get to the most important line items, let me first touch on the property-related and general and administrative expenses. The property-related expenses relate to ground leases and other expenses (taxes and insurance, to name a few) that MPW pays directly. The total amount is reimbursed by the tenants. So this number won't affect MPW, only from a short-term liquidity standpoint. Regarding the G&A expenses, the biggest contributor to the decline in these line items is because of fair-value adjustments. All of which is already incorporated in the NFFO numbers that I touched on before.

What we are getting is (finally) some top-line growth in the rent-billed line item. Growing by a very meaningful 7.3% quarter-over-quarter. From this +/- $12 million in growth, around $7.6 million is coming from new tenants that took over Steward post-bankruptcy. The rest should probably be attributed to rent escalators.

The fact that NFFO didn't grow in Q2, despite a rapid increase in 'rent billed' is because of the enormous $2.5 billion refinancing effort that took place this year. Impacting interest expenses quite a bit. But those interest expenses are not increasing next quarter, revenue is...

So if new tenants keep ramping up payments and future tenants that take on operations of Prospect Medical Group come in, (Ceteris Paribus), we should see a pretty meaningful increase in both the top-line as well as the bottom-line. In this bullish scenario, the stock will definitely creep back up.

I want to end this part of the article with the dividend coverage. Indeed, dividends are not covered by free cash flow. Subtracting non-cash revenue (straight-line rent) from NFFO will get you to $0.07 per share ($0.14 - $0.07). And this excludes any capital expenditures that go towards new (small) equity investments, like the one in Infracore (around $61 million dollars at today's exchange rate). Meaning that management is expecting dividends to be covered very soon. Coming from new tenants ramping up payments and the Prospect resolution. You can argue about management's decision to keep paying a dividend at a negative payout ratio, but since MPW is not planning to expand its portfolio or something, you could argue that it is a logical move to keep paying a dividend. Since (Ceteris Paribus) the dividend will be covered by free cash flow rather sooner than later.

MPW's Debt Picture (And The JV's)

Due to the impact of fair value adjustments (for example) and the limited transparency that Joint Ventures (JVs) have, it's difficult to draw a precise picture of all that is happening. Instead, I try to identify broad trends rather than pin down exact figures.

When we take a look at the (non-covenant) debt metrics, we can at least see that MPW's financial leverage (total debt as a percentage of total gross assets) is more or less the same.

It's important to see how this financial leverage will develop in Q3, especially since the Prospect bankruptcy process is currently about to wrap up.

But perhaps even more important are the Joint Ventures. Let's calculate the financial leverage on these JV's. Especially how this metric is trending. The relevance of doing this is related to the fact that MPW's management has increased its equity investment in the Infracore JV this quarter. And no matter the reason behind it, I believe that management has lost so much trust that any investment they make feeds into the negativity. So let's take a look.

In order to compare MPW's own leverage to the JV's leverage, we need to calculate all of this using the same formula. In calculating its financial leverage, Medical Properties Trust defines total gross assets as follows:

When we take a look at the JV's, we get the following financial leverage:

  • Q1 2025: $1,070,938 / ($2,462,449 + $320,045) x 100% = 38.5%
  • Q2 2025: $1,171,760 / ($2,784,268 + $367,250) x 100% = 37.1%

As you can see, the JV's leverage is lower and decreasing. These are positive signs.

Not only are the Joint Ventures more conservatively leveraged, the leverage is also declining. But as we have seen with both Steward and Prospect, a lot of investments were not retrieved when both these tenants went belly up. So just as important is the operational performance of the JV's.

I focus mainly on operational costs: property-related, interest, and G&A. Since these are - more or less - cash expenses that strongly correlate with operational performance. When we take those three line items as a percentage of total revenue, we get the following trend:

  • Q1 2025: ($2,057 + $12,136 + $613) / $38,512 x 100% = 38.4%
  • Q2 2025: ($2,366 + $13,200 + $807) / $42,704 x 100% = 38.3%

Again we see a declining percentage of revenue going towards expenses.

So, from the Joint Ventures front (at least on the surface), I don't see deteriorating fundamentals; both from a leverage and an operational performance perspective.

Excluding Infracore (which owns and leases 18 Switzerland facilities, excluding two facilities currently under development), all of the underlying JV assets are fully leased. Yes, that equals to 100%. Since we have no occupancy number related to Infracore, the following part of the conference call might be worth mentioning. A 21% year-over-year revenue growth seems very good.

How are MPW's new tenants really doing?

Sure, revenue is ramping up, so we could say that tenants that replaced Steward are doing fine. But a lot of more skeptical investors have pointed out that these new operators are – on average – low-tier operators. So let's start by taking a look at what MPW's management had to say:

From what I can see (compared to Q1 2025 --> chapter: How are [new] tenants doing?) things are currently trending nicely...? Even College Health and Tenor Health (both not mentioned in the CC) seem to be on track:

When looking at the financial support that Sharon Regional Medical Center (or Tenor Health, who is now operating it) received, we can see real evidence that hospitals are critical real estate infrastructure where U.S. citizens rely on. Sure, we should not forget the predatory practices of PE-firms (like Steward) that financially drained those hospitals for personal gain. But there was COVID, a huge spike in labor costs and overall inflation that shut down capital markets and ballooned interest expenses. Not even talking about medical reimbursements that need time to catch up. It was a perfect storm that management couldn't foresee. What we can do is look at the current trends regarding company performance. And as far as I can see right now: Medical Properties Trust seems to be recovering.

Risks (And Opportunities)

Medical Properties has been a really bad investment for many investors. Luckily I was late to the party to buy into this REIT when all the screaming buys were flying around. I started a small position at $12 and bought my last batch around the $3 range. My average purchase price sits around $6.35. So I am still sitting on a loss (including dividend payments) like many of you.

For now, I am here to keep a very close eye on all (public) developments that are currently unfolding before our eyes. Especially the Prospect story will be very interesting to follow in the coming week.

The sale and leaseback business approach that Medical Properties Trust relied on for years, has attracted predatory PE firms that drained hospitals and left after nothing was left anymore. This systemic problem can only be cured by robust legislation. Until then, REITs that still rely on this structure will trade at discounted fair values going forward. But at the current $4.05 price, the stock is still being punished beyond what I think is reasonable. Even at the depressed Q2 2025 NFFO ($0.14), the annualized P/NFFO multiple is 7.1x. The average multiple comes in at 11.43x. Pre-COVID, the multiple even reached higher than 16x. But those were different times, so you should never expect MPW to go back to those levels anytime soon. But 7.1x at these depressed levels while at the same time we see early signs of recovery? I'm not convinced that +/- $4 a share is reasonable.

Medical Properties Trust seems to be in a turnaround phase. Risks associated with the Prospect chapter 11 outcome are real and unknown for now. So it is hard to speculate on that. Before August 11th, we should learn more regarding this chapter 11 development. Until then, I don't see a clear reason to panic. At a very low P/NFFO multiple that is about to grow rapidly if new tenants keep ramping up payments and post-Prospect tenants are taking over operations, the upside potential could still be huge. And even if something goes wrong, cutting the dividend will still prevent Medical Properties Trust to go under.

I want to close this chapter by saying that shorting Medical Properties Trust is dangerous. Yes, you could say that management are all bandits that are here to ruin this company. Or that an enormous amount of hospitals will go bankrupt (where do those patients go, by the way?) But let's be fair, this is pure and unproven speculation. At the current valuation, there is no logical reason to short Medical Properties Trust, based on fundamental trends and the overall outlook. You can be more bearish or skeptical, but going short on this company is another story. The upside is limited (if the stock goes to $0) but the downside is limitless.

This is not going to be your new Enron or Wirecard. So even though the shorts could be right theoretically (management are crooks that have manipulated the numbers for years), the odds are much higher that fundamentals keep improving than that things are starting to fall apart again. If you want to gamble, go to a casino. You have a higher change of winning there than you have in shorting Medical Properties Trust. With a 35.5% of the float shorted (as of June 30th) it is becoming really risky...

Closing Remarks

I will probably be here until the share price reaches $7. Since I don't trust MPW's management (there is no recent track record to do so), selling (most) of my position when management gets rewarded themselves, makes a lot of sense to me. Until then, my core focus is on the trends and developments regarding portfolio performance and tenant health (including Prospect's unfolding endgame).

I started covering Medical Properties Trust back in December last year. Calculating the intrinsic value using REIT specific metrics. If I see a steady operational trend in the coming quarters, I will go back to my fundamental valuation calculations. But for now, it's way more useful to myself and you fellow investors to focus on more important metrics that will influence the stock price: portfolio stability and tenants that pay rent.

Thanks for stepping by. I appreciate it.

data.title: Got Summer Plans? Canadians Are Keeping It Local And Boosting Small Businesses
data.description:
With summer travel in high gear, Canadians have been hitting the road. But this year many have been choosing to spend a lot of their travel dollars close to home. Julia Kelly, Vice President of Small Business Banking at TD, discusses a recent TD survey showing how Canadians are buying into domestic tourism and supporting small Canadian businesses in the process.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell - With summer travel season in high gear, Canadians are hitting the road and choosing to spend a lot of their travel dollars closer to home. A recent TD survey shows Canadians are buying into domestic tourism and supporting small Canadian business in the process. Here to talk us through the trends that she's seeing is Julia Kelly, Vice President of Small Business Banking at TD. So great to have you with us, Julia.

Julia Kelly - Thank you. Thank you for having me.

Greg Bonnell - Let's start with the big picture. We do have a lot of economic uncertainty. We've been living with it for a while now. But it's summertime. Canadians still want to travel. What stood out the most for you in these findings?

Julia Kelly - You know, it's terrific news for small business owners across Canada because we learned nearly 9 in 10 Canadians said that supporting small businesses was important to them. Canadians have not put their travel plans on hold, with 60% of Canadians planning to travel within Canada this summer.

Greg Bonnell - Now, I understand the findings, too, and this is interesting to me because usually people say, I got vacation plans. It's a big location. I'm going to go -- I went to Banff earlier this year. Some say, I'm going to the east coast. I'm going to go to Quebec City.

Canadians, nearly 3/4 of them saying they're willing to travel just to visit a unique local business, whether it's a restaurant, a shop, a quirky attraction. What does that tell you about the consumer and their behavior right now?

Julia Kelly - Well, it's really a shift in traveling domestically. 64% of Canadians are planning to travel within Canada over the summer months. And there's been a keen interest in supporting local Canadian businesses. 9 in 10 have said it's important to them to support local. And keeping in mind, summer travel doesn't always mean traveling across the country. Sometimes it might be staying pretty close to home, traveling in their home province.

And while visiting unique businesses is important to them, what is evident in the trend is that when planning a trip, they're making a point to research, whether that's through social media or through different outlets and community advertisements, visiting local attractions like restaurants who have a specific or unique flavor or businesses that are really specialized to that local area. People are looking for a unique experience, something they can't get anywhere else.

Greg Bonnell - So here is opportunity for these small businesses. Also, imagine some challenges. How would this -- I'm thinking of a seasonal community, a rural community start to tap into this demand for this experience to grow their reach?

Julia Kelly - So we know 73% of Canadians will consider a travel destination based on this unique attraction they might have. And so, really, it's about staying true to what you do well as a unique business in a local economy. So when you think about that, it might be - what is specific to that area? What might be something that is grown or unique or not able to get anywhere else, especially outside of a city? And something that might be a bit quieter that is an attraction for a summer plan and you can't get during the winter months.

Greg Bonnell - I was telling you before we came on air that in the spring, I went to Banff. It was my 25th wedding anniversary. Despite the economic uncertainty, I had to go big because it was my 25th wedding anniversary. But we do have this uncertainty, people keeping a little tighter hold on their money. Is this shaping the way Canadians are choosing to travel, choosing to spend?

Julia Kelly - You know, the uncertainty has been trending for a while, and we've heard from a lot of small business owners regarding concern around consumer spending and slowing down of spending. So learning that tourism will pick up across the country has really been welcome news, especially when we look at small businesses that are subject to seasonality. And the summer months, with increase in local traffic and this initiative to support local businesses, will carry not just through the summer months but even into the fall. So it's exactly what we wanted to hear - that Canadians are looking to support local small businesses as part of their summer vacation plans.

Greg Bonnell - So when you speak to these small businesses across the country and the owners, which I know you do, what are they telling you about this environment and how they're adapting to it?

Julia Kelly - Small business owners are one thing. They are resilient. And I think there are Canadians who have really shown how important it is to them to support these local small businesses. So as we've seen through the survey results, we know that there is uncertainty, and they're taking time to think intentionally on how to mitigate the impacts of uncertainty, adapt their businesses to attract more customers, and work with local suppliers to understand how they can really impact their bottom line, especially if they're seeing an influx of local travelers.

Greg Bonnell - In Canada, of course, summer has all too short a life. We head into the fall and the winter. Perhaps some of these businesses won't see the kind of support just simply because we're back at work and we're not traveling as much. What kind of support or advice do you think some of these businesses will need to keep thriving and evolving in this new landscape?

Julia Kelly - I think it's important for small businesses to really understand - get in touch with your advisor. At TD, we keep the dialogue open with our clients. We want small business customers to succeed, and I encourage customers to meet with your small business advisor to have those discussions around cash flow management. What are their options? How are they thinking about advice when they are faced with uncertainty? And together, we can proactively find solutions. And so keeping that dialogue open is really critical as they look forward into an uncertain landscape.

Greg Bonnell - Obviously, you have a line of communication with these business owners. I told you, I was surprised by the idea that it wasn't about the city. It was about the unique, quirky restaurant or some other location. What surprised you most out of these survey findings?

Julia Kelly - What surprised me most was the amount to which people will research their trip and looking into local attractions, doing the research ahead of time to know what is that one specific attraction across the city that is really going to drive someone's interest. And there are so many different interests that people have. So the ability to tap into people's interests and the unique attractions really stood out.

Original Post

data.title: Trade And OBBB Deals Sealed, Fed Dread, And AI/Meme Dreams
data.description:
As the stock market reached new all-time highs, investors had plenty to juggle—both in Washington, D.C., and on Wall Street. The S&P 500 climbed +2.2%, the NASDAQ surged +3.7%, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average edged higher by +0.1% for the month.

The One Big Beautiful Bill

What has fueled the rally? A major catalyst was President Trump’s signing of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBB) on July 4th. The nearly 900-page legislation spans a broad range of economic issues including tax reform, healthcare, energy policy, and national security.

According to the Congressional Budget Office (CBO), the combined impact of tax cuts, new spending, and spending reductions will result in a net cost of $3.4 trillion over 10 years (see chart above). Supporters of the bill argue that this projection underestimates the long-term stimulative effects of tax relief and strategic investments. Whether the deficit widens as the CBO projects, or narrows thanks to a stronger, growing economy, remains to be seen.

Trade Deals Sealed

Since Liberation Day on April 2nd, trade negotiations have progressed unevenly. The administration’s reciprocal tariff hikes were paused through August 1st to allow final agreements to be reached. Following months of tough rhetoric, multiple major trading partners ultimately signed deals before the deadline—including the European Union, Japan, the United Kingdom, Vietnam, and South Korea—thereby avoiding punitive tariffs.

Talks with our two key trading partners, Mexico and Canada, remain ongoing. While Mexico was granted a 90-day extension amid constructive dialogue, Trump slapped a 35% tariff on Canada (from 25%) due to what the White house said was “continued inaction and retaliation.” The tariff pause with China stops on August 12th.

Here’s a list of the new country tariffs released by the president late yesterday.

Regardless of all the tariff uncertainty, investor sentiment improved last month as the terms of the signed deals were significantly milder than originally feared.

Adding to the optimism:

  • Core inflation in June remained modest at 2.8% (Reuters), and
  • Tariff revenues collected through July reached $126 billion, beating initial estimates (Politico) – see chart below. Strategist Ed Yardeni forecasts that 2025 tariff revenues could surge to between $400 billion and $500 billion (Barron’s).

Fed Dread

Of course, when it comes to financial markets, everything can’t just be rainbows and unicorns without something for investors to worry about—and this month, a key concern remains Federal Reserve policy. Critics, including the president, argue that interest rates are too high, with the Federal Funds Rate currently set at 4.25%–4.50% (Yardeni Research) – see chart below.

By comparison:

  • The European Central Bank’s Deposit Facility Rate stands at 2.00%, and
  • The Bank of Japan’s overnight rate is only 0.50%.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell has held off on further cuts, citing the need for more clarity on inflation and labor market data, especially in light of recent tariffs. Ironically, when the Fed last cut rates by -1.00% late last year, the 10-year Treasury yield rose by roughly +1% (see chart above), reflecting fears of rising inflation.

This week, the Fed held rates steady for the fifth consecutive meeting (YouTube). Notably, two FOMC members—Christopher Waller and Michelle Bowman—dissented, voting in favor of a rate cut. It was the first dual dissent by Fed governors in over 30 years—a clear signal of division inside the central bank.

Meme Dreams

With the major indexes at new highs, speculation has returned in full force. Money-losing, struggling companies like Opendoor Technologies (OPEN), GoPro Inc. (GPRO), and Kohl’s Corp. (KSS) saw their shares double, triple, or even quadruple over a short span (WSJ) – see chart below. We saw similar trends occur during the GameStop and AMC meme craze in 2021.

Adding fuel to the fire:

  • Cryptocurrency prices are on the rise again.
  • Euphorically priced IPOs (Initial Public Offerings) like Figma, Inc. (FIG), which more than tripled in value ($115 per share) on its first trading day above its offering price ($33 per share) valuing the company above $50 billion – more than 30 times next year’s forecasted revenues.
  • SPACs (Special Purpose Acquisition Companies)—often criticized for poor governance—are staging a comeback.

Combined, all these trends raise concerns about froth, which investors have experienced at previous peaks.

Climb in AI Stocks Persists

No discussion of this rally would be complete without highlighting the AI mega-cap giants. Companies like Alphabet (GOOGL) (Google), Meta (META) (Facebook), Microsoft (MSFT), and Amazon (AMZN) all recently announced capital expenditures for 2025 that will likely exceed an astounding $350 billion —most of it allocated to AI infrastructure.

Meanwhile, NVIDIA Corp. (NVDA), the AI-chip juggernaut and major beneficiary of all the AI capex, has seen its share price soar+63% in just three months, reaching a staggering $4.4 trillion market value.

Valuations High but Fundamentals Remain Strong

While stock valuations remain elevated above historical averages (the S&P 500 red line trades at 22x forward earnings, according to Yardeni) – see chart above, the macro backdrop remains supportive:

  • The economy is strong,
  • Unemployment is low,
  • Corporate profits are growing, and
  • Monetary policy may turn more accommodative in coming months.

In this momentum environment, the market should continue its productive juggling, but if the frothy or economic winds worsen, investors should be prepared for a dropped ball.

Disclosure: Sidoxia Capital Management (SCM) and some of its clients hold positions in GOOGL, META, AMZN, MSFT, NVDA, and certain exchange-traded funds (ETFs), but at the time of publishing had no direct position in OPEN, GPRO, KSS, GME, AMC, FIG or any other security referenced in this article. No information accessed through the Investing Caffeine (IC) website constitutes investment, financial, legal, tax or other advice nor is to be relied on in making an investment or other decision. Please read disclosure language on IC Contact page.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: JOET: High-Turnover Factor Duo Underperforms The S&P 500, Skeptical View Merited
data.description:
Today's note is supposed to offer an update on the Virtus Terranova U.S. Quality Momentum ETF (JOET), an exchange-traded fund that I assigned a Hold rating to in all three notes presented since November 2021.

And this article is not supposed to mark a sharp turn in my sentiment, as I still see neither a confidently bullish nor bearish case for it. I continue to support the viewpoint that JOET has a sound strategy that does deliver on its factor promises (there is quality and there is momentum in its portfolio), but it is not potent enough to outmaneuver the S&P 500 consistently, and I will offer data to support that point below.

What Is JOET's Strategy?

As we know from JOET's website, the core of its strategy is the Terranova U.S. Quality Momentum Index, which is rebalanced quarterly. The ETF's turnover is definitely high at 99%.

As described in the fact sheet, the index creation starts with "the 500 largest U.S. companies" that are screened for positive momentum measured using "the last 12 months’ total return." When 125 names that failed that test are removed, phase two starts when candidates face a quality test: the composite score used in assessment includes "return on equity, debt to equity, annualized sales growth rate over the past three years." 125 companies of the highest quality join the index and are weighted equally.

As to the trio of quality parameters assessed, I would like to highlight the following:

  • Thanks to the ROE ingredient in the composite score, JOET's weighted average figure (adjusted for triple-digit and negative values) stands at 23.65% vs. 20.63% of the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), as per my calculations.
  • Its exposure to companies with debt/equity above 100% is 19.06% vs. IVV's 26.67%.
  • And its weighted average 3-year revenue growth rate is 13.8% vs. IVV's 13.3%.

However, this did not make JOET considerably growthier or of much higher quality (with other metrics like ROA and ROTC factored in) than IVV. I will address that shortly.

A remark must be made that I like strategies that go beyond the cliches of the value-growth dichotomy and experiment with other factors in their pursuit of alpha. I stated this in the past, and I reiterate it today: JOET's momentum/quality duo makes a lot of sense. Momentum is a time-tested factor that is about benefiting from stocks that have been on a tear. The problem is that momentum stocks, especially when their growth prospects look spectacular but current fundamentals are poor, can crater as fast as they were climbing higher, or even faster. And the quality factor that focuses on reality (i.e., balance sheets, capital efficiency, etc.) and not just expectations should counterbalance that, making performance smoother and more appealing from a risk perspective, potentially leading not only to outperformance versus the S&P 500 in absolute terms but also to higher risk-adjusted returns. But has it been the case with JOET? There is a lot to discuss here.

JOET Returns Discussion

The picture here is mixed. First, optimists who would for some reason like to defend a bullish viewpoint can start with JOET's YTD performance that has been unquestionably robust. The fact is, this year, amid tariffs, concerns about monetary policy that, for some investors, might look dangerously restrictive, prospects of a recession, and geopolitics, the market has been going through phases of anxiety and despair, intertwined with rush and buoyancy, all with a touch of FOMO. But after all, the S&P 500 ETF still gained 6.8%, as the buy-the-dip tactic has been winning again and again.

While this return is about 2.2x lower than its result in 2024 over the same period, this is quite impressive, especially with the April sell-off factored in.

However, despite these challenging conditions, JOET has bested not only IVV but also such quality-centered ETFs as the iShares MSCI USA Quality Factor ETF (QUAL), the Invesco S&P 500 Quality ETF (SPHQ), and the JPMorgan U.S. Quality Factor ETF (JQUA), though it has lagged the Fidelity Quality Factor ETF (FQAL).

Sure, I must admit that this is an excellent result. However, we must zoom out and look at the returns since inception (or on the December 2020–July 2025 period, to be precise). And the data we see here tells a different story, as JOET is no longer an outperformer.

Rather, it is a highly volatile ETF that has one of the weakest annualized returns in the group, as it captured over 111% of IVV's downside but less than 100% of its upside. Risk-adjusted returns (Sortino and Sharpe ratios) also do not speak in its favor. It did outperform the Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP) (and JOET compares its returns to both the S&P 500 and its equal-weighted version), but I do not regard this as a particularly impressive achievement.

And just to give a bit more color, in November, I guessed that "the chances that the ETF will outperform IVV in 2024 have increased" thanks to "the election-related upside volatility." Nevertheless, it was my miscalculation, as, alas, JOET actually underperformed IVV in 2024. And it was its third full calendar year of underperformance and second in a row.

Year JOET Total Return IVV Total Return
2020 3.73% 3.78%
2021 26.80% 28.76%
2022 -18.04% -18.16%
2023 16.37% 26.32%
2024 24.00% 24.93%
2025 8.81% 8.52%

Created by the author using data from Portfolio Visualizer

JOET Portfolio

But performance is not everything. There is another equally important aspect I consider when deciding whether an ETF is worth buying into, and it is factor mix.

Here, I should say that JOET does deliver two things: it has quality characteristics that are complicated to criticize and solid momentum. However, IVV is still ahead on a few fronts, as the table below illustrates.

More specifically, while about 35.2% of JOET's holdings have been replaced since November 2024, most conclusions that could have been drawn from its November factor exposures are still valid.

For example, with 125 equities in the portfolio, JOET does have a footprint in the trillion-dollar league (7 companies in total, key among them being Microsoft (MSFT) and NVIDIA (NVDA)), but the equal weighting makes their impact on its factor profile and returns much smaller than on IVV's, and this is reflected in the weighted average market cap and, in part, in its adjusted earnings yield.

As to quality, nearly all stocks in the portfolio have a B- Quant Profitability grade or higher. And momentum stocks account for 75.66% of it, and while the S&P 500 ETF is also overweight in them, JOET still has a larger allocation.

However, IVV offers much more appealing metrics when it comes to forward revenue, EBITDA growth, and capital efficiency (Return on Assets and Return on Total Capital, something JOET's overexposure to financials detracted from).

Sector JOET IVV
Financials 34.8% 13.8%
Information Technology 18.2% 34.0%
Industrials 16.6% 8.6%
Consumer Discretionary 7.8% 10.4%
Health Care 6.3% 8.8%
Communication 5.6% 9.9%
Utilities 2.5% 2.5%
Real Estate 2.5% 2.0%
Consumer Staples 2.4% 5.2%
Energy 1.6% 3.0%
Materials 0.8% 1.8%

Created by the author using data from the ETFs. Weights as of August 1 for JOET and July 31 for IVV

Final Thoughts

A skeptic in me prevails this time again. JOET is a good product with a smart strategy, a modest expense ratio of 29 bps, healthy returns, and a nice factor balance. But for me, this is a definition of a fund that qualifies for a Hold rating. I see nothing particularly outstanding here, convincing enough to construct a bullish thesis, especially considering that JOET has been incapable of beating IVV since its inception. So all in all, this is an interesting ETF to keep an eye on, not to buy into.

data.title: TSYY: High Risk Leveraged Income From Tesla
data.description:
Overview

I've covered many different option ETFs in the past that can generate massive levels of income, and the list of funds available to investors continues to grow larger. GraniteShares YieldBOOST TSLA ETF (TSYY) is one of the newest high-yield synthetic option ETFs to hit the market. TSYY aims to provide indirect exposure to Tesla (TSLA) and places an emphasis on income generation through its option writing strategy. The fund only launched back in December of 2024 and has a management fee of 0.99%. I've covered a similar fund, YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (TSLY) in the past, but TSYY is an amplified version of TSLY.

Looking at the performance since inception, we can see that TSYY's share price has declined by about 63.7%. The fund has been on a continuous decline and I don't anticipate that the ETF will see a recovery. This is because TSYY offers investors a massive estimated annual distribution rate of approximately 140%! I believe this distribution rate is unsustainable and will continue to drain the fund's NAV over time. Not to mention, Tesla hasn't seen the strongest earnings reports either, so the overall sentiment of the company is negative. Additionally, the total return still sits at a loss of 26.3% when including all distributions paid out to shareholders.

I've often talked about how this sort of high yield synthetic ETFs can be utilized to create massive income streams and how the large yield can repay investors their initially invested capital. Additionally, TSYY is unique because it pays out the distributions on a weekly basis, which can be appealing for investors looking to create frequent income generation. However, TSYY's rapid decline and focus on leverage makes me less certain about the long-term sustainability of this ETF. The fund has been around for less than a year and the share price has almost exclusively declined. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that TSYY implements to generate its income.

Fund Strategy

TSYY operates a synthetic option strategy using Tesla as its focus. The primary goal is to generate income, so investors should not use TSYY as a way to maintain common exposure to the price movements of Tesla. The term 'synthetic' means that TSYY doesn't hold any common shares of Tesla, so it is writing options against an asset that it doesn't actually own. This can substantially increase the risk profile of the fund as the share price can be severely disconnected from the daily price change of Tesla.

The fact sheet states that TSYY also amplifies the strategy by focusing on 2x leveraged ETFs. By exposing itself to a 2x leveraged ETF that gets 200% of the daily percentage of common shares, TSYY has an amplified level of volatility to implement its option writing strategy. Higher volatility can lead to higher option premiums that are collected, which is how TSYY can offer such a large annualized distribution rate.

TSYY utilizes put options on a levered Tesla ETF and they are cash secured, which means that TSYY holds a sufficient amount of capital to cover losses if the options are exercised. The fund also implements an OTM (out-of-the-money) strategy and put options are sold with strike prices that are between 10% to 40% above the current market price. The options typically have an expiration period that is between one week to one month out, which allows the fund a sufficient amount of time to collect premium income. I suggest reading through the prospectus since it lays out the exact strategy that is implemented.

However, the prospectus states that the fund will also buy further OTM puts to limit the level of losses that the ETF can experience. However, the fund's operating history doesn't indicate that TSYY has been able to achieve lower downside risk. Looking at a quick price comparison between TSYY and TSLA, we can see how TSYY has declined twice as much as common shares of Tesla. Therefore, I want to put emphasis on the fact that this ETF is only for high risk investors that want to collect income. Additionally, timing around a positions entry is important here; I would only initiate a position once I see that the overall sentiment and outlook on Tesla shifts more positive.

If you are bullish on the outlook of Tesla, you are likely better off owning the common shares of TSLA. By choosing an income fund like TSYY, you are actively deciding to prioritize income generation instead of total return. The fund's inclusion of an option strategy means that the growth potential will always be capped, despite using an OTM approach. If TSLA sees some strong quarterly earnings in the future, the TSYY is likely to underperform.

Just to help visualize how the strategy works, let's imagine that TSYY is writing options against a TSLA ETF priced at $100 per share. TSYY may write options and select a strike price of $110 per share. If the ETF sees strong upside movement to $115 per share, TSYY will only participate in the growth up to the selected strike price. So all growth above the strike price is essentially sacrificed in exchange for the premium that is collected.

Performance Risk

Despite TSYY's rapid decline since inception, the fund's distributions have been able to provide a higher total return than common shares of TSLA. For instance, TSYY's total return since inception, including distributions, results in a loss of 26.3%. However, common shares of Tesla have declined by nearly 30% over the same time frame. Despite the slightly lower loss, it's still a bit challenging to suggest a position in TSYY due to the larger capital erosion.

Additionally, I believe that the leveraged nature of the fund strategy presents a greater amount of risk compared to an alternative fund like YieldMax's TSLY. Just to demonstrate, I pulled in a performance comparison on a YTD basis between the two ETFs. As we can see below, TSYY's share price has declined by more than 63.6%, while TSLY's share price has declined by only 46.5% over the same period. However, TSLY has a smaller estimated annual distribution rate of approximately 65.9%. So TSLY offers an annual distribution that is less than half the amount of TSYY's annual yield.

Despite the lower yield that TSLY offers, this alternative ETF has maintained a lower total loss on a YTD basis of 24.7%, including all distributions paid. TSYY has a total loss of 26.1% on a YTD, which is close to TSLY, but the much larger capital erosion makes it a lot less attractive in my opinion.

Many of these synthetic option ETFs share similar vulnerabilities, and TSYY seems to be no exception. It's an unfortunate trait of holding a synthetic option ETF that caps the upside price potential, while still exposing shareholders to all of the downside risk. A capped upside price potential means that these funds simply cannot recover from market downturns as efficiently. I have kept close tabs on many of the option ETFs that have launched over the last year, and I've noticed that this is a common issue with nearly all of the funds with extremely generous distribution rates.

GraniteShares does have other YieldBOOST funds that we can compare against, but many of them were launched less than a quarter ago, so their performance metrics are skewed. However, I plan to revisit these funds over the next 6 months to provide some updates on their performance.

  • GraniteShares YieldBOOST NVDA ETF (NVYY)
  • GraniteShares YieldBOOST Bitcoin ETF (XBTY)
  • GraniteShares YieldBOOST SPY ETF (YSPY)
  • GraniteShares YieldBOOST QQQ ETF (TQQY)
  • GraniteShares YieldBOOST COIN ETF (COYY)

Since the operating histories of those ETFs are so short, I thought I'd pull from different examples of other synthetic option ETFs. Although their underlying strategy and approach may be different, these funds operate similar option strategies, and the point is to demonstrate that they all tend to decline over time. The inclusion of an option strategy makes it very difficult for these funds to recover from downward swings in the market, since their upsides are capped. Looking at the performance of the following ETFs below, we can see that they've all suffered from declines. You'll notice that the ETFs that held up a bit better, were the ones that aim to track an underlying index or operate as 'fund of funds'.

  • YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (TSLY)
  • Defiance Nasdaq 100 Enhanced Options & 0DTE ETF (QQQY)
  • Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (QDTE)
  • YieldMax Universe Fund of Option Income ETFs (YMAX)

Distribution Utility

What makes TSYY unique compared to other peers is that the distributions are paid out on a weekly basis. The latest declared distribution is $0.2368 per share, which brings the annual distribution yield up to 140%. Since new distributions are declared every single week, the annualized yield can look different based on the time you are reading this.

The fund originally paid out its distributions on a monthly basis, but shifted to a weekly basis in June. As we can see below, the payout amounts can change every week and are determined based on the fund's underlying performance, success of the written options, volatility levels, and overall momentum of Tesla. Therefore, TSYY isn't the best choice for investors that are looking for a more reliable source of income.

When we're talking about an annualized yield of 140%, it is entirely possible for investors to receive their original invested capital back in the form of distributions. So some quick napkin math tells us that $10,000 invested into TSYY is estimated to generate ~$14,000 in annual dividend income. Therefore, it's possible for an investor to achieve 'house money' status, which is the cross point where all dividends received are greater than the amount of capital invested.

I went back and manually calculated all distributions that were paid out since the fund's inception. The breakdown is as follows:

2024: 1 payout of $0.0465 per share

2025 (YTD): $9.8231 in total distributions paid

Total: $9.8696 in total distributions paid since inception.

The fund launched around a share price of $20 per share. If we assume that an investor initiated a position around the time of inception, an investor would have now received nearly half of their initial invested capital back in the form of distributions. However, one of the benefits of the fund is that the distribution seems to be 'tax-efficient'. For instance, the latest Section 19 (a) notice indicates that 100% of the distribution was funded by return of capital. Return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore, aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale.

However, the caveat is that all distributions aren't guaranteed to be made up of return of capital. Each week the distribution makeup can be different and may include a significant amount of net investment income. Therefore, there is still a risk that distributions from TSYY may have tax consequences that investors may need to account for. Additionally, return of capital distributions can directly impact the NAV of the fund, which exacerbates the issue of a declining share price.

Takeaway

In conclusion, it is a bit difficult to suggest a position in TSYY at this time. The ETF has demonstrated that it offers very little downside protection and the large distributions may exacerbate the large share price decline. Although the weekly income may seem attractive for income investors, I don't think the risk of continuous capital erosion is worth the tradeoff. However, risky investors may be enticed by the large annualized distribution of 140%. This large yield makes it possible to collect your initially invested capital back in the form of distributions. Additionally, the return of capital classification also means that the overall tax liability can be limited.

data.title: Trex: A Cautious Upgrade Is Warranted
data.description:
Things have not been going well for shareholders of Trex Company (NYSE:TREX) over the last few years. Back in November of 2021, I decided to dig into the company. Even though I acknowledged at the time that it was a quality firm that had attractive long-term prospects, I also argued that shares looked 'drastically overpriced'. Based on valuation alone, I decided to rate it a 'sell', reflecting my view at the time that the stock would likely underperform the broader market for the foreseeable future.

So far, that call has proven to be successful. While the S&P 500 is up 36%, shares of this business have dropped by 41.7%. Overall financial performance for the firm has actually improved during this time, even though revenue has barely increased. This year, we are seeing some pain, with revenue, profits, and most cash flow metrics, all declining because of weak market conditions. Having said that, management seems optimistic about the rest of the year. Unfortunately, because of how shares are priced on an absolute basis and compared to other similar firms, I do not think that it makes sense for shareholders to buy into. But I would argue that now might be a good time to upgrade it, just barely, to a 'hold' rating.

One thing that I find to be particularly interesting is that while management is optimistic about this year, analysts are less so. You see, after the market closes on August 4th, management will be reporting financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that time, analysts believe that revenue will increase marginally year over year. In fact, their estimates put overall sales as being a bit above the midpoint of what management expects for the quarter. But on the bottom line, profits are expected to decline. Obviously, if we do end up seeing a downward revision compared to what management has indicated is likely for the year, a downgrade wouldn't be difficult to justify. But for now, I am holding to my new neutral assessment.

Time For A Cautious Upgrade

According to the management team at Trex Company, the company operates as the world's largest manufacturer of composite decking and railing products. This is an interesting niche in the home building market. And as the industry leader in the space, as well as a provider of certain fencing solutions, outdoor furniture products, and even unique offerings like cornhole boards and spiral stairs, it is certainly the kind of business that appeals to me.

There is plenty to love about this industry. For instance, it is a huge market. According to management's own estimates, the total outdoor living category for the US is worth around $75 billion. This includes certain smaller spaces like the $8 billion decking and railing category, and the $2 billion composite decking and railing space. All told, Trex Company has a 13% market share of the broader outdoor living category for the country.

There's also the opportunity for the business to grow in the long run. The specific total addressable market opportunity for decking, railing, and adjacent markets, is estimated to be around $13.7 billion. And some of these spaces are incredibly fragmented and open the door to growth by means of acquisition. For instance, management says that the railing market, which is worth $3.3 billion, is highly fragmented.

At this time, Trex Company has a 6% market share in this space, making it the second largest competitor in the industry. All combined, the top ten largest players in the industry control only about 33% of the space.

Obviously, acquisitions are one way to grow. But management is not content with just that. The firm continues to make innovations, such as in 2024 when it initiated the national launch of Trex Signature, a high-quality decking platform that management describes as both durable and luxuriously crafted. Other such launches aimed at achieving organic growth over the years have included Trex Transcend, Trex Select, and Trex Enhance, with these different product lines targeting customers of all walks of life. Even smaller markets are being focused on. For instance, management is currently focusing on the $250 million market opportunity that includes butt joint clips, decking and fascia screws, and fascia plugs dedicated to decking.

While the primary focus of the company is on its operations in the US, it is also incredibly focused on other markets as well. It has about 12% of the market in the UK. It controls about 1% of the market in Australia, 4% in France, and 7% in Germany. All combined, the business sells its products in over 40 different countries, and in most of those, it has less than 1% market share.

Financial performance for the business in recent years has not been great. But I wouldn't call it awful either. From 2022 through 2024, revenue inched up from $1.11 billion to $1.15 billion. All of this growth and more came from the 2023 to 2024 fiscal year when revenue grew by 5.2%. Management claimed that this was because of changes to its early buy program that helped to boost revenue.

On the bottom line, the picture has generally gotten better. Over the last three years, net profits expanded from $184.6 million to $226.4 million. The increase in adjusted net profits was from $204.2 million to $226.4 million. Operating cash flow did drop from $216.2 million to $143.9 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, it inched up from $273.8 million to $280.1 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $312.8 million to $360.3 million.

For the 2025 fiscal year, the enterprise has experienced some weakness. Revenue, for instance, dropped from $373.6 million in the first quarter of 2024 to $340.3 million in the first quarter of 2025. Management blamed this on a drop in volume, further detailing that the first quarter of last year included $40 million in channel inventory build that did not reoccur this year. In essence, customers decided to frontload inventories last year but did not do the same this year, even as the company believes that this season will be quite busy.

Naturally, this drop in sales would bring with it lower profitability. Net income contracted from $89.1 million to $60.4 million. On an adjusted basis, the drop was from $89.1 million to $64.4 million. Operating cash flow did improve from negative $174 million to negative $154 million. But on an adjusted basis, it ended up contracting from $103.4 million to $77.1 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business dropped from $133.2 million to $101.3 million. Management mentioned that, in addition to declining sales, the firm dealt with gross profit margin contraction caused by railing conversion costs, the impact that lower volumes have on economies of scale, and changes in the production process aimed at improving its entry level enhanced decking boards.

At first glance, it might seem as though investors should be worried for this year. Tentatively, however, there are reasons to be optimistic. Even though the housing industry is struggling because of a decline in newbuilds caused by inflationary pressures and high interest rates, Harvard currently expects that, by the end of this year, the trailing 12-month repair and remodeling activity in the US will be 1.8% above what it was a year earlier.

For its part, management believes that revenue will climb by between 5% and 7%. They also expect an EBITDA margin in excess of 31%. Even if we take the 31% figure here, that would mean EBITDA of $378.4 million compared to the $360.3 million the company reported a year earlier. Similar growth rates when it comes to other profitability metrics would mean adjusted net income of $237.8 million and adjusted operating cash flow of $294.2 million.

If we use these estimates, and we also use the historical results for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. Honestly, these are really high multiples. In fact, they are pretty close to the point of deserving a bearish assessment. Fortunately, relative to other similar firms, shares are priced at reasonable levels. In the table below, I stacked it up against five other similar enterprises. On both a price to earnings basis and on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, I found that three of the five companies I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is. And on a price to operating cash flow basis, it ended up being the third-cheapest of the group.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Trex Company 29.9 24.1 19.9
Zurn Elkay Water Solutions Corporation (ZWS) 43.8 24.7 21.5
AAON Inc. (AAON) 44.0 307.3 30.8
Fortune Brands Innovation (FBIN) 16.0 10.4 10.6
Simpson Manufacturing (SSD) 22.9 19.6 15.4
Armstrong World Industries (AWI) 27.8 27.0 16.1

In the table below, you can see estimates that analysts have provided for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year and how those compare to results from 2024. Management had indicated that overall revenue for the quarter would be between $370 million and $380 million. Analysts believe it will be toward the higher end with sales of $377.6 million compared to the $376.5 million reported for the second quarter of 2024. Unfortunately, earnings per share and adjusted earnings per share are both expected to decline. If that does happen, you can bank on the idea that the cash flow metrics shown in that table will also drop year over year. But with management so far being optimistic, I would be a bit surprised if analysts end up being right when it comes to the bottom line results.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I do think that Trex Company is an interesting company. But shares are still very expensive. The good news is that they aren't so expensive as to warrant the bearish assessment that I have had leading up to this point. While certain headwinds might persist, especially throughout this year, I do think that upgrading it to a very soft 'hold' rating is appropriate at this time.

data.title: Apellis Pharmaceuticals, Inc. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Apellis Pharmaceuticals, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Why Everyone's Talking About Private Credit In 2025
data.description:
As markets adjust to new tariff policies, private credit is gaining traction. VanEck shares insights on why this asset class is in sharp focus.

Key Takeaways

  • Private credit surges in 2025 as banks retreat from lending and investors seek higher, less correlated yields. Projections suggest it could hit $2.8 trillion by 2028.
  • Middle-market companies and sectors like healthcare and tech are turning to private lenders for flexible financing.
  • ETFs, like VanEck’s BIZD and GPZ, are expanding retail access to private credit strategies with daily liquidity and diversified exposure.
  • The combination of structural growth drivers, macro tailwinds, and new access pathways are paving the way for private credit to evolve into a mainstream income allocation for both institutions and individual investors.
  • In the second half of 2025, focus is shifting to refinancing needs ahead of the 2026–2027 maturity wall in high-yield bonds and leveraged loans.

Demand for private credit continues to surge, with projections suggesting it could hit $2.8 trillion by 2028.1 Private credit has stepped into the spotlight in 2025, and it’s not hard to see why. Amid volatile markets, evolving trade dynamics, and investors’ growing appetite for alternative income, private credit offers something rare: the potential for attractive risk-adjusted returns and yield. Here’s what’s fueling the boom and why more investors are adding it to their portfolios.

What’s Driving the Private Credit Boom?

The 2023 regional banking crisis accelerated the pre-existing trend of banks retreating from lending to private companies that tend to be below investment grade or not rated. This void is being filled by private credit. Unlike banks, which are subject to stricter regulations and capital requirements, private lenders offer more flexibility and can tailor loan structures to meet the specific needs of borrowers. This flexibility, coupled with their increasing pool of capital, positions private credit as a crucial source of financing, particularly for businesses that may not meet the stricter criteria of traditional banks.

More recently, the macro backdrop of 2025 has been a perfect catalyst for private credit’s rise. Volatility across traditional stocks and bonds has prompted investors to seek for assets less tied to public market swings. At the same time, geopolitical tensions and new tariff policies are reshaping global trade, tightening corporate borrowing conditions, and creating demand for non-bank lending alternatives.

Private Credit End of Year 2025 Outlook

The momentum behind private credit isn’t expected to slow. In 2025, specifically, there are several tailwinds for private credit:

  • Middle-market companies often underserved by traditional banks are increasingly turning to private lenders to finance growth, acquisitions, and recapitalizations.
  • Sector-specific opportunities in industries like healthcare, technology, and infrastructure are expanding.
  • Institutional allocations continue to grow, with pension funds and insurers viewing private credit as a core income strategy rather than a niche alternative.

However, investors should also be mindful of risks: tighter financial conditions could pressure weaker borrowers, and while the market is expanding, manager selection will remain critical.

Why Investors Are Paying Attention to Private Credit

A sustained spread premium continues to exist between public and private markets, allowing investors to earn higher yields for lending privately. This spread advantage, combined with the evolving debt landscape, has created a rich opportunity set for private credit lenders.

Recent years have seen a major shift: instead of relying on traditional syndicated loan markets, many companies have turned to private credit for refinancing needs. Looking ahead, attention is shifting toward a looming maturity wall, as a significant portion of high-yield bonds and leveraged loans are set to come due in 2026 and 2027. In contrast to past cycles, this maturing debt could see even greater private market participation as companies seek flexible financing solutions outside of traditional bank lending.

At the heart of private credit’s appeal are three core benefits:

  • Attractive yields: Private loans often offer higher income than comparable public bonds, a compelling advantage in a higher-rate world.
  • Reduced correlation to traditional stocks and bonds: Private credit tends to be less sensitive to day-to-day market swings, helping diversify portfolios.
  • Potential inflation protection: Many private credit deals feature floating-rate structures that adjust with rising rates.

Access to private credit is expanding. Historically reserved for large institutions, private credit is now increasingly available to individual investors through new vehicles, such as ETFs. This democratization is reshaping the landscape, but with it comes the need for careful attention to credit quality, illiquidity risks, and manager expertise.

The Role of Private Credit in a Diversified Portfolio

Is private credit here to stay? All signs point to yes. The rise of private credit is part of a broader rethinking of portfolio construction. As traditional fixed income struggles to deliver the same returns and diversification benefits it once did, investors are seeking alternative income sources. Private credit fits the bill, offering a differentiated return stream while complementing traditional assets and investment strategies.

Institutions are leading the way, often carving out dedicated allocations to private credit within their multi-asset portfolios. Retail investors are following suit, thanks to more accessible vehicles. VanEck believes that private credit will play a strategic role in diversified portfolios moving forward. However, investors should carefully weigh liquidity needs, risk tolerance, and manager quality when making allocation decisions.

Accessing the Market: Private Credit ETFs and BDCs

Traditional private credit investments, while offering the potential for attractive returns and yield, come with a drawback: illiquidity. Unlike publicly traded stocks or bonds, these investments often involve long lock-up periods, typically several years. This means your money is tied up for the duration, inaccessible for immediate needs or strategic portfolio adjustments. This inflexibility can be a major hurdle for investors who require more dynamic access to their capital, especially in a market rife with uncertainties. In an environment where liquidity is highly prized, Business Development Companies (BDCs) present a compelling, liquid alternative to traditional private credit strategies.

BDCs bridge the gap between traditional private credit and publicly traded securities. BDCs offer the same benefits as traditional private credit strategies – the potential for higher yields and diversification away from traditional stocks and bonds, but in a much more liquid form.

ETFs like the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) offer diversified exposure to leading BDCs, which in turn invest across a wide range of private loans. For investors seeking access to private credit opportunities without the barriers of direct lending, including capital commitments, illiquidity, and operational complexity, BIZD provides a streamlined alternative.

Importantly, these vehicles allow investors to participate in private credit’s growth potential while maintaining daily liquidity, transparency, and simplicity.

Investing in the Firms Powering Private Markets

Another way to tap into the growth of private credit is by investing in the companies that operate and manage the funds behind it. These are alternative asset managers, firms that provide financing through private credit while also overseeing capital across private equity, real estate, infrastructure, and venture capital. As the organizations sourcing deals, structuring loans, and allocating private capital, they are positioned to benefit directly from the increasing demand for private market solutions.

While investors have long been able to buy shares of publicly listed alternative asset managers, the role these firms play in portfolios is evolving. As demand for private credit and other private market strategies grows, these companies are expanding their business models. Many are reaching beyond institutional capital and into wealth management and retirement channels, broadening their investor base and solidifying their importance in today’s investment landscape. With access to long-term capital and resilient fee structures, they are well positioned to benefit from the continued shift toward private markets.

To help investors access this opportunity, VanEck has introduced the Alternative Asset Manager ETF (GPZ). This ETF seeks to track as closely as possible, before fees and expenses, the price and yield performance of the MarketVector Alternative Asset Managers Index (MVAALTTR), which is intended to track the overall performance of alternative asset managers across private equity, venture capital, private credit, private real estate, and private infrastructure.

Unlike direct private investments, GPZ offers diversified exposure to the firms driving the expansion of private markets. As private markets reshape global capital flows, alternative asset managers have become central to this transformation. GPZ gives investors a way to participate in that growth through a single, liquid, and transparent ETF.

What Comes Next for Private Credit?

As the private credit market matures, several themes are likely to shape its future:

  • Regulation: Increased attention from regulators may lead to more oversight, especially around risk management and disclosure.
  • Growing demand: New entrants, from large asset managers to fintech platforms, are making private credit more competitive and innovative.
  • Portfolio integration: Private credit is becoming a core portfolio allocation, not just a niche play.

Looking ahead, VanEck sees private credit evolving into a mainstream income strategy for both institutions and individual investors. The combination of structural growth drivers, macro tailwinds, and new access pathways positions private credit as a critical part of the investing conversation — not just in 2025 but well beyond.

Important Disclosures

1 Source: Prequin, Moody’s Ratings, Private Credit Global Outlook. As of January 2025.

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. MarketVector Alternative Asset Managers Index is the exclusive property of MarketVector Indexes GmbH (a wholly owned subsidiary of Van Eck Securities Corporation), which has contracted with Solactive AG to maintain and calculate the Index. Solactive AG uses its best efforts to ensure that the Index is calculated correctly. Irrespective of its obligations towards MarketVector Indexes GmbH, Solactive AG has no obligation to point out errors in the Index to third parties. The VanEck Alternative Asset Manager ETF is not sponsored, endorsed, sold or promoted by MarketVector Indexes GmbH and MarketVector Indexes GmbH makes no representation regarding the advisability of investing in the Fund.

Investing in listed alternative asset managers may be speculative and involve substantial risks, including leverage, liquidity, significant volatility, operational complexity, valuation, limited public information, and the risk of borrower default or bankruptcy. Small, medium and large-capitalization companies may be subject to elevated risks.

Business Development Companies (BDCs) generally invest in less mature U.S. private companies or thinly traded U.S. public companies which involve greater risk than well-established publicly-traded companies. While the BDCs that comprise the Index are expected to generate income in the form of dividends, certain BDCs during certain periods of time may not generate such income. The Fund will indirectly bear its proportionate share of any management fees and other operating expenses incurred by the BDCs and of any performance-based or incentive fees payable by the BDCs in which it invests, in addition to the expenses paid by the Fund. A BDC’s incentive fee may be very high, vary from year to year and be payable even if the value of the BDC’s portfolio declines in a given time period. Incentive fees may create an incentive for a BDC’s manager to make investments that are risky or more speculative than would be the case in the absence of such compensation arrangements, and may also encourage the BDC’s manager to use leverage to increase the return on the BDC’s investments. The use of leverage by BDCs magnifies gains and losses on amounts invested and increases the risks associated with investing in BDCs. A BDC may make investments with a larger amount of risk of volatility and loss of principal than other investment options and may also be highly speculative and aggressive. The Fund and its affiliates may not own in excess of 25% of a BDC's outstanding voting securities which may limit the Fund's ability to fully replicate its index. An investment in the Fund may be subject to risks which include, among others, investing in BDCs, investment restrictions, financial sector, small- and medium-capitalization companies, equity securities, derivatives, derivatives counterparty, liquidity risk related to swap agreements, floating rate risk for BDCs, market, operational, regulatory, index tracking, authorized participant concentration, no guarantee of active trading market, trading issues, passive management, fund shares trading, premium/discount and liquidity of fund shares, issuer-specific changes, and index-related concentration risks, all of which may adversely affect the fund. data.title: Credit Rating For The Unrated REITs (Part 15): DiamondRock Hospitality
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield

Today's pick for our series credit rating for the unrated REITs with DiamondRock Hospitality Company (DRH).

Company Overview

Founded in 2004 and headquartered in Maryland, DRH is a REIT with a portfolio of geographically diversified hotels and resorts, with approximately 36 properties and 9595 rooms. The total market capitalization of the company is close to $1.6 billion.

The common stock is at a $7.72 market price. The forward "price to FFO" ratio is 7.70, so the expected future FFO yield comes to 12.9%.

DRH has an asset coverage ratio of 263%. The generated rental yield is approximately 24%, and operating expenses (excluding depreciation) account for around 90% of total revenues. The interest expenses are around 5.5% of the total debt. When calculating the asset yield (by dividing total revenues by total asset value), we obtain a value of 30%. However, for a more accurate assessment, a market-based adjustment is applied using the price-to-book value (P/B) ratio, which is currently 1.02. After the market adjustments, the calculated asset yield is 29.88%, and the market-adjusted asset coverage ratio is 266%.

Preferred Stock

DRH has one preferred stock:

(NYSE:DRH.PR.A) is with a current market price of $25.31 and a current yield of 8.18%. Its call date is on 08/31/2025, and it has a 6.62% yield to call, or yield to worst. It is not rated by the major credit agencies. DRH issued 4.4 million shares of DRH.PR.A on 08/20/2020. The stock pays cumulative distributions of 8.25% per annum, or $2.06 annual amount, in the form of $0.515625 per quarter.

Credit Score

Let's now look at the credit rating, using, as always, Moody's methodology:

  1. Gross assets. DRH has close to $3.2 bln in gross assets, so it falls into the Baa3 category.
  2. Unencumbered assets represent 87% of all assets, which gives it a score of A3 again.
  3. Secured debt for the company stands at 9%, which is in the A3 category.
  4. Total debt + preferred stock as a percentage of gross assets. The company’s total debt and preferred stock comprise about 31% of its total assets. That ratio corresponds to a credit rating comparable to Baa1.
  5. Net debt-to-EBITDA. We use the total debt here, and with an EBITDA of $279.5 million, the ratio is close to 4.6, or a score of 8.4, equivalent to Baa1.
  6. Fixed charge coverage. EBITDA/(interest and dividend payment on its preferred stock). EBITDA covers interest and preferred stock dividends close to 4 times, and this is a Baa2 credit score.

Based on these six scores, which we call "the balance sheet scores," DRH gets an overall balance sheet score of 7.6, or a Baa1 credit rating.

Since it is a hotel REIT, Moody's rates this type of rate for the next 2 points with one of the worst ratings. Here:

-Market Positioning And Asset Quality

-Operating environment

We give a DRH credit rating of B3.

Liquidity And Access To Capital

The company's operating expenses, excluding depreciation and amortization, are about 90% of revenue. EBITDA covers interest and dividends on preferred shares nearly 4 times. Based on these indicators, all other things being equal, DRH deserves a credit rating equivalent to a Baa3 credit score.

After calculating a score for market positioning, operating environment, and liquidity and adjusting all ratios based on the market-adjusted characteristics, we get a credit score of 10.2, or a solid Baa3 credit score.

Conclusion

DRH is a hotel REIT with a well-diversified portfolio and a strong market presence. Although operating in a riskier sector, its financials demonstrate good debt management, high asset coverage, and decent profitability. Based on Moody’s methodology, the company deserves a Baa3 credit rating, which is the lower end of investment grade – stable, but with some risks due to the sector in which it operates.
